derivation: BODMAS = Brackets, Orders, Division, Multiplication, Addition, Subtraction; restricted to the four listed operators that is Div, Mult, Add, Sub = option B. (Real C precedence puts * / % at one level above + -, consistent with B.)
Compiled and ran with gcc: the trailing ';' makes the for body empty, loop exits with i=6, printf runs once -> output '6' = option D (verified by execution, not inference).
First octet 11 (binary 00001011, leading bit 0) lies in 1-126 -> Class A -> default mask 255.0.0.0 = /8, 8 network bits / 24 host bits.

4.0.0.0 -> Class A, default /8. Mask 255.255.255.0 = /24, borrowed = 24-8 = 16 -> 2^16 = 65,536 subnets; host bits = 32-24 = 8 -> 2^8-2 = 254 usable hosts. Confidence <1 only because older ip-subnet-zero-excluded textbooks would say 65,534 subnets; the modern (2^borrowed) convention used here is the one the whole q022/q023/q024 set is internally consistent with (q023 would otherwise give 0 subnets).
198 in 192-223 -> Class C, default /24. 255.255.255.252 (last octet 11111100) = /30, borrowed = 30-24 = 6 -> 2^6 = 64 subnets; host bits = 2 -> 2^2-2 = 2 usable hosts (classic point-to-point /30).
A database is by definition an organized collection of interrelated records; spreadsheet/text file are storage tools and MIS is a system built on a database.

Standard MS-Access-flavoured item: 'Unlike filters, queries can be saved as OBJECTS in a database' - a query persists as a database object, a filter is transient. Stem is missing its blank placeholder (reads 'saved as in a database') but the intended blank and answer are unambiguous.
Records physically ordered by a key field = sequential file organization / sequential access method; direct (random) access computes addresses and imposes no stored order.

Of the four options only 'database created using a DBMS package' is actually a database, so A by elimination. WEAK ITEM: in real terminology an 'external database' is one residing outside the organization (e.g. a commercial online data service), which none of the options states; the stem/options pairing is crowd-sourced and semantically poor, though A is still the only defensible key.
Negative stem: DML = INSERT/DELETE/UPDATE/SELECT (options B, C, D). CREATE TABLE is DDL, so it is the one thing DML is NOT used for.
A minimal superkey (no removable attribute) is a candidate key; the primary key is merely the candidate key chosen by the designer.
A query nested inside another query's WHERE/HAVING clause is a subquery (nested query); the other three are not SQL terms.

A data dictionary holds metadata (names, types, constraints, where data is located); the physical size of the disk storage device is a hardware fact, not metadata -> B. NOTE: options C and D ('allows a many-to-many relationship', 'is conceptually simple') are corrupted fragments imported from a different question (network data model advantages); they are nonsense here but do not create ambiguity, so the key still holds. Worth repairing the distractors.
Data hierarchy = field < record < file < database; the field is the smallest of the listed entities processed as a single unit.
